title: Paper clay lidded box
subtitle: A slab-built box with a fitting lid, sized for jewellery or small keepsakes
excerpt: A small slab-built box with a lid that rests on a rebate: a stepped inner rim that locates the lid without a hinge or clasp. Paper clay holds the corners without cracking and tolerates the close tolerances needed for a fitting lid.
sourceNotes: Slab-built box construction derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: Clay with cellulose fibres mixed in, which strengthens joins and reduces cracking; especially useful for slab boxes where corner joins are under stress.
The drying stage where clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to join and trim.
Scratching joining surfaces and brushing them with slurry before pressing together.

projectSchedule.title: Build box body and let walls firm
projectSchedule.body: Assemble the four walls and base and leave to reach leather-hard overnight before adding the rebate strip.

projectSchedule.title: Add rebate strip and build lid
projectSchedule.body: Attach the inner rebate strip to the box top, and cut and fit the lid slab. Let the whole assembly dry for two more days.

content.text: A lidded
paper clay

box requires accurate slab cutting, careful corner joining, and a rebate strip: a thin inner rim that the lid rests on. The cellulose fibres in paper clay make it more tolerant of the thin walls and tight joins that a fitting lid demands. Target box size: 10 cm by 7 cm by 5 cm tall.
Cutting the parts
Roll a slab to 6 mm thickness. Cut the following pieces with a fettling knife against a ruler:
Base: 10 cm by 7 cm
Two long sides: 10 cm by 5 cm (each)
Two short sides: 5.8 cm by 5 cm (each, allowing for the wall thickness on both long-side ends)
Lid: 10 cm by 7 cm (same as the base)
Rebate strip: 8 mm wide and long enough to run inside all four walls, about 28 cm total; this can be cut in sections
Assembling the box
1. Join the walls to the base
Use the
score-and-slip

method on every join. Start with the two long sides, standing them up on the base, their outer faces flush with the base edge. Then join the two short sides into the gaps. Reinforce each inside corner with a thin coil of clay, blended flush with a finger.
2. Let the box firm to leather-hard
Cover loosely with plastic overnight. The box body needs to be
leather-hard
before the rebate strip is added; adding it while the box is still soft deforms the walls.
3. Attach the rebate strip

The rebate strip runs around the inside of the box, 3 mm below the top rim. Score the inside wall surface and slurry it. Press the strip firmly in place and smooth the inside face. This strip is the shelf the lid rests on.
4. Fit the lid

The lid slab should sit on the rebate strip with 1-2 mm clearance on each side. Check the fit while the box and lid are both still at leather-hard: if the lid is too wide, trim with a fettling knife; if the fit is correct, leave. Do not join the lid to the box; it should lift off freely.
Drying and finishing
Dry the box and lid separately on a flat board for two to three days. Once bone-dry, sand smooth and seal with PVA inside and out. The sealed box holds jewellery, small keys, or other dry keepsakes.

items.symptom: Corners crack as the box dries
items.cause: Walls were at different moisture stages when joined
items.fix: Press paper clay slurry into the crack; cover the box loosely with plastic to slow drying

items.symptom: Lid does not fit after drying
items.cause: Shrinkage pulled the walls inward as the clay dried, tightening the opening
items.fix: At leather-hard stage, gently press the top rim outward slightly; allow for 5-8% shrinkage when cutting the lid
